summary: I was born in Singleton County, Georgia, on May 6, 1866, and I practically lived there all my life. My parents, Josh and Susan Robinson, raised sixteen children there, though only five of us remain now. Growing up, I did chores like cutting wood and attending cows, and I remember helping my mother every Wednesday with her work. Singleton County holds all my early memories and the foundation of my life.

My brother Boyers lives in Sandersville, Georgia, and my first wife, Kattie Jackson, died there. I haven't heard from some of my siblings in years, but Sandersville is one place where family ties remain. It’s a part of my life story, connected to family and loss.
cues: first wife Kattie Jackson died there

summary: My sister Anna lives in Warthen, Georgia, and I also worked for the Grit L company there, earning $7.50 a week. I held that job until I got tired of it before moving on to other work. Warthen was a place where family and work intersected in my younger days.

When I was twenty, I left home and went to live with my father's sister in Davisboro, Georgia, but only stayed three weeks. That brief stay was part of my early journey away from Singleton County. Davisboro was a short chapter in my life as I sought my path.

My mother often talked about slavery, recalling how she was fourteen when freedom was declared. She told stories of slaves being sold and whipped, and how she had to sleep on moss on hard clay floors. Those were hard times, and her experiences shaped my understanding of our history and struggles.
cues: stories of selling and whipping slaves

summary: Last year, I attended the WPA Adult School at the Peace Casena, hoping to improve my reading and writing. Unfortunately, I didn't learn much because the young teachers weren't very advanced, so I quit. Still, I believe education is important and necessary for better jobs and life.

The Peace Casena was where I went to the WPA Adult School last year. It was a place for adult education, but I found the instruction lacking and decided to stop attending. Still, it represents an opportunity for learning that I tried to take advantage of.

I worked for the Southern Railway Company at Mitchell, Georgia, for three years, starting around 1919 when I was about three or four years old. That was one of my early jobs, and I earned 75 cents a day back then. It was honest work that helped me make a living.

Mitchell, Georgia, is where I worked for the Southern Railway Company for three years around 1919. It was part of my early working life in Georgia before I moved on to other jobs. Mitchell holds memories of my first earnings and labor.

I worked for the Grit L at Warthen, Georgia, making $7.50 a week. I stayed in that job until I got tired of it and moved on to sawing wood, where I earned more. The Grit L was a stepping stone in my working career.

After moving to Florida, I worked for the Cummer Lumber Company in McClenny, making ties at 10 cents each. Some days I earned as much as five dollars. McClenny was where I continued my labor after leaving Georgia.

I worked for the Cummer Lumber Company at McClenny, Florida, doing piece work making ties. I was paid 10 cents a tie and sometimes made as much as five dollars a day. This job was part of my transition from Georgia to Florida work life.

I married in Lakeland, Florida, in 1927 and later settled there working various jobs. I worked for American Express and the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad in Lakeland, and now I am clearing land for a white man with a large orange grove in the south section of the city. Lakeland has been home for many years and a place where I built my life.

I worked for the American Express Company for five years in Lakeland, Florida, earning $40 a month. It was a steady job before I moved on to work for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad. American Express was part of my working history in Florida.

After American Express, I worked for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad packing boxes and later as a helper to the machinist. Eventually, I was promoted to fireman earning $210 a month until they rolled me off, meaning I was cut off from work. The railroad was a big part of my working life in Florida.

Beck is my trusty old mule, fourteen years old and a good worker. I use Beck to help me make a living, averaging eight to ten dollars a week. Beck is like a partner to me, and I say if people obeyed like Beck, things would be better. I take care of Beck and depend on her for my daily work.

I was moved by a speech Iagoose Bethune made about being decent, clean, and having a clean place to live. His words stuck with me because I believe in decency in all things, especially religion. Iagoose's message reinforced my values about cleanliness and respect.

I belong to the Free Will Baptist Church, pastored by Rev. Williams on Fourth Street. Religion is important to me, and I try to live decently and cleanly as the church teaches. The church community is part of my spiritual life, though I see some foolishness among people sometimes.
cues: member of Free Will Baptist Church

summary: I feel that leaving Jesus Christ out of my life would be wrong. I don't believe in using religion to weigh a person down, but I do believe in living rightly and following common sense, as Jesus taught. My faith is a guiding force in how I live and treat others.

I admire David for being strong and going on his way, and I try to live like that. His example shows how a man should be steady and true, which is how I want to be. I often think of David when considering how to face life's challenges.

Lakeland, Florida, is where I married in 1927 and where I have lived and worked many years. I have a home there with no electricity, relying on lamps for light, and I work clearing land for a northern white man’s orange grove. Lakeland is my current home and the center of my life now.

Kattie Jackson was my first wife, and she taught me how to read and write because I had little schooling. She died in Sandersville, Georgia, and we had one child who died three weeks after birth. Kattie was a smart woman who helped me learn and was part of my early family life.
cues: taught me to read and write

summary: Seathas is a grocery man in North Florida with whom I have a bargain to know a ten-acre tract for half of what I raise. I have considered this deal but must wait until I finish my current contract. Seathas represents a potential opportunity for me to expand my farming.

The Ladies' Birthday Almanac was among the old books I keep on a two-deck book rack in my home. These books are part of my modest possessions and reflect some of the reading material I have had over the years. They are a small but meaningful part of my household.
